abstract.text: This article describes the KeyboardKit localization engine.

inlineContent.text: A flexible localization engine is an important part of a keyboard, where every supported locale should be able to localize the keyboard and its texts, keys and secondary callout actions.
In KeyboardKit, each
defines localized strings, assets, and locale-specific information. KeyboardKit also extends the native
type with additional capabilities.
unlocks localized keyboards and services for all the locales in your license. Information about Pro features can be found at the end of this article.
KeyboardKit has a
enum that defines all supported keyboard locales. It’s also a namespace for locale-related types and views, like the
KeyboardKit supports
keyboard-specific
s, like
, and
A keyboard locale refers to a native
and has localized assets and strings that can be translated with
. KeyboardKit also extends the native
type with a bunch of additional capabilities.
has provides collection modifiers, behaviors, flags, search & sorting capabilities, and much more. For instance, you can get the
of a locale, translate the name of a locale with other locales, etc.
Many extensions are applied to
instead of
, whenever the base locale has enough information.
will for the most cases not mirror these extensions, so use the
property to access them.

inlineContent.text: The

You can also use

You can automatically add a context menu to the keyboard by inserting a

content.text: How to change the primary language

inlineContent.text: Setting the
will update the controller’s
primaryLanguage
, which controls things like spell checking and text direction. This also sets the keyboard’s language subtitle in the keyboard switcher.
The
property seems to always return
, even after it has been set properly (and works). This can be a bit confusing, but just check that the proper language is displayed in the system keyboard switcher.

content.text: How to use LTR and RTL locales

inlineContent.text: KeyboardKit supports LTR (Left-To-Right) and RTL (Right-To-Left) locales if you use the
. Just change the locale as explained above, and KeyboardKit will automatically adjust the text direction, spell checking, etc.
If you want to use a single RTL
without using KeyboardKit, you can adjust the
Info.plist
file in your keyboard extension like this:
Set
PrefersRightToLeft
to
Set
PrimaryLanguage
to the language code of your locale, e.g.
fa
for Perian (Farsi).
Just be aware that setting the primary language like this may affect external keyboard mappings if you don’t set the primary language.
Each
has a
file in
Resources/[id].lproj
with texts that are applied with the
enum.
For instance, this translates the numeric button key for the current locale:

or

functions, which either take

values:
Besides localized strings, you can use the context
to set how locales are translated for a keyboard.
If you need to use a locale (language) that KeyboardKit doesn’t support yet, like Japanese or Simplified Chinese, you can either create a custom
configuration to support it, or reach out to implement a new
To change the keys of the keyboard to fit an unsupported locale, you must implement a custom
and
, where an input set defines the input keys and a layout defines the full layout. See the
article for more information.
To change the texts of the system keys, you must create a custom
(although this will change to be easier in KeyboardKit 9). See the
article for more information.
To change the secondary callout actions that show when long pressing certain keys, you must create a custom
. See the
article for more information.
Finally, you must also implement your own
, which most probably will involve integrating with a lightweight model that doesn’t take up too much memory, since keyboard extensions are limited to ~70MB.

content.text: How to help KeyboardKit Pro support more locales

inlineContent.text: If you have expertise on a certain locale that you want KeyboardKit to support, you can reach out to help adding it to KeyboardKit Pro.
If a locale gets added as a result of your contribution, you will get a free Basic license or a discount on higher plans. See the website for more infromation on how to get in touch.
The
namespace has locale-specific views, that can be used to perform native locale operations, pick locales, etc.
has a
that can be used to change the
from a callout context menu:

action, as well as to the space bar, if the

You can also add this menu to any view with the
view modifier.
unlocks more
s, a
and a
for every locale in your license.
This lets KeyboardKit create a fully localized
for every locale, with no additional code needed. You can customize any part for any locale at any time, whenever needed.
After setting up KeyboardKit Pro with your license key, as described in the
article, you can just change the
to automatically update the keyboard view for any locale in your license.
